item.title: High-pressure experimental constraints of carbonates + pyrite ± water interactions: implications for coupled C-H-O-S cycling in subduction zones
item.dc:creator: Jintao Zhu
Renbiao Tao
Yuhang Lu
Takayuki Ishii
Wenqing Sun
Lifei Zhang
item.description: Carbonates, alongside pyrite and hydrous minerals, are the primary carriers of volatile elements (i.e., carbon-hydrogen-oxygen-sulfur) on sediments and altered oceanic crusts. Their interactions under high-pressure, high-temperature conditions in subducting zones are essential to understanding the coupled volatiles cycle. In this study, we investigated the interactions between different carbonates (e.g., calcite/aragonite, magnesite, and dolomite) and pyrite under both dry and hydrous conditions at pressures ranging from 3.5 to 5 GPa and temperatures from 1000 to 1500 °C using a large volume press. Combined with related thermodynamic modeling, we found that under dry conditions, sulfidation of Ca 2+ -carbonate and Fe 2+ -carbonate generates oldhamite (CaS) and pyrrhotite (FeS), respectively, along with CO 2 and O 2 . The limited thermodynamic stability fields of oldhamite and niningerite (MgS) make pyrrhotite the main sulfidation product on Earth. In contrast, under hydrous conditions, the stability of pyrite is significantly decreased by water (H 2 O), resulting in the formation of reduced pyrrhotite, H 2 S, and oxidized S-bearing species (e.g., SO 4 2- ) simultaneously. Furthermore, some reduced carbon species (e.g., graphite and hydrocarbons) are abiotically produced from the reduction of carbonates and/or water by the above H 2 S. Therefore, we propose: (1) sulfidation of carbonates by pyrite can cause release of oxidized fluids from subducting slabs, further driving local oxidation of the mantle wedge; (2) the carbonates-pyrite - water reaction is a new mechanism for the formation of abiotic graphite and hydrocarbons (HCs) in subduction zones. Published: 2026-03-04T16:18:11+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.667 (must_read)

Authors: Jintao Zhu; Renbiao Tao; Yuhang Lu; Takayuki Ishii; Wenqing Sun; Lifei Zhang

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123348

Relevance: 0.667 (must_read)

Carbonates, alongside pyrite and hydrous minerals, are the primary carriers of volatile elements (i.e., carbon-hydrogen-oxygen-sulfur) on sediments and altered oceanic crusts. Their interactions under high-pressure, high-temperature conditions in subducting zones are essential to understanding the coupled volatiles cycle. In this study, we investigated the interactions between different carbonates (e.g., calcite/aragonite, magnesite, and dolomite) and pyrite under both dry and hydrous conditions at pressures ranging from 3.5 to 5 GPa and temperatures from 1000 to 1500 °C using a large volume press. Combined with related thermodynamic modeling, we found that under dry conditions, sulfidation of Ca 2+ -carbonate and Fe 2+ -carbonate generates oldhamite (CaS) and pyrrhotite (FeS), respectively, along with CO 2 and O 2 . The limited thermodynamic stability fields of oldhamite and niningerite (MgS) make pyrrhotite the main sulfidation product on Earth. In contrast, under hydrous conditions, the stability of pyrite is significantly decreased by water (H 2 O), resulting in the formation of reduced pyrrhotite, H 2 S, and oxidized S-bearing species (e.g., SO 4 2- ) simultaneously. Furthermore, some reduced carbon species (e.g., graphite and hydrocarbons) are abiotically produced from the reduction of carbonates and/or water by the above H 2 S. Therefore, we propose: (1) sulfidation of carbonates by pyrite can cause release of oxidized fluids from subducting slabs, further driving local oxidation of the mantle wedge; (2) the carbonates-pyrite - water reaction is a new mechanism for the formation of abiotic graphite and hydrocarbons (HCs) in subduction zones.

item.title: Rise of oxidation and hydration in Neoarchean crust-building magmas caused by subduction (North China Craton)
item.dc:creator: Yuan-Yuan Liu
Chao Wang
Mark B. Allen
Hongyu Zhang
Li Su
Shuguang Song
item.description: The Neoarchean (2.8–2.5 Ga) represents a critical transition towards a habitable Earth. Gradual development of plate subduction during this era facilitated crustal differentiation and maturation, reflected by compositional changes of the Archean continental crust. However, key physical properties of Neoarchean crust-building felsic magmas, such as oxygen fugacity ( f O 2 ) and water content, remain loosely constrained. Here we show that ca. 2.5 Ga granitoids in the North China Craton are considerably oxidized and hydrous, and comparable to modern arc magmas. Their elevated f O 2 and water content (ΔFMQ −1.0 to 2.8, 5.7–16.6 wt% H 2 O; average at ΔFMQ +0.8 and 9.6 wt% H 2 O) are attributed to fluids released from the subducting oceanic crust, which metasomatized the mantle wedge. Such slab-derived fluids facilitated the generation of Neoarchean oxidized and hydrous felsic magmas through water-fluxed melting of the underplated mafic crust derived from the metasomatized mantle wedge, accompanied by various redox reactions and volatile incorporation. Our work demonstrates that localized synchronous oxidation and extensive fluid circulation across Earth's reservoirs driven by plate subduction likely occurred in some cratons at the end of the Archean. Published: 2026-03-06T23:54:07+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.634 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuan-Yuan Liu; Chao Wang; Mark B. Allen; Hongyu Zhang; Li Su; Shuguang Song

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123359

Relevance: 0.634 (must_read)

The Neoarchean (2.8–2.5 Ga) represents a critical transition towards a habitable Earth. Gradual development of plate subduction during this era facilitated crustal differentiation and maturation, reflected by compositional changes of the Archean continental crust. However, key physical properties of Neoarchean crust-building felsic magmas, such as oxygen fugacity ( f O 2 ) and water content, remain loosely constrained. Here we show that ca. 2.5 Ga granitoids in the North China Craton are considerably oxidized and hydrous, and comparable to modern arc magmas. Their elevated f O 2 and water content (ΔFMQ −1.0 to 2.8, 5.7–16.6 wt% H 2 O; average at ΔFMQ +0.8 and 9.6 wt% H 2 O) are attributed to fluids released from the subducting oceanic crust, which metasomatized the mantle wedge. Such slab-derived fluids facilitated the generation of Neoarchean oxidized and hydrous felsic magmas through water-fluxed melting of the underplated mafic crust derived from the metasomatized mantle wedge, accompanied by various redox reactions and volatile incorporation. Our work demonstrates that localized synchronous oxidation and extensive fluid circulation across Earth's reservoirs driven by plate subduction likely occurred in some cratons at the end of the Archean.

item.title: Paleoelevation variations and exhumation history of the Tibetan Plateau control spatiotemporal distribution of porphyry system deposits
item.dc:creator: Peng Zhang
Zhenjie Zhang
Mark J. Hoggard
Qiuming Cheng
item.description: Porphyry deposits, due to their significant size and economic value, have long been a focus of research. Nevertheless, the majority of studies concentrate on the genesis of porphyry deposits, with relatively few considering how post-formation preservation conditions go on to influence their present-day distribution. The unique geographical setting of the Tibetan Plateau makes it a key region for investigating the interplay between ore deposits, tectonic deformation, and geomorphic evolution in an intraplate setting. In particular, its abundant tectonic and magmatic events have facilitated the formation of numerous porphyry deposits. This study combines big datasets of whole-rock geochemistry, zircon geochronology, and low-temperature thermochronology to reconstruct the tectonic and magmatic evolution, paleoelevation variations, and exhumation history of various terranes within the Tibetan Plateau since Jurassic times and explores how these processes relate to the formation and preservation of porphyry deposits. Our results show that the Qiangtang terrane contains a higher and more clustered concentration of porphyry Cu-Au deposits during intervals of decreasing paleoelevation, whereas the Lhasa terrane predominantly hosts porphyry Cu-(Mo) deposits during intervals of increasing paleoelevation. We propose that intense tectonic and magmatic activity is a critical factor in the formation of porphyry deposits, while significant fluctuations in the paleoelevation of terranes also favor their development. Conversely, regions of the Tibetan Plateau with high degrees of exhumation host fewer porphyry system deposits, and their deposits tend to be of smaller tonnage. This indicates that post-formation exhumation plays a critical role in the preservation of porphyry deposits, with intense exhumation likely leading to erosional loss of previously formed deposits. Areas with lower exhumation volumes should therefore be prioritized as key targets for future mineral exploration. Published: 2026-03-04T21:03:41+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.630 (must_read)

Nevertheless, the majority of studies concentrate on the genesis of porphyry deposits, with relatively few considering how post-formation preservation conditions go on to influence their present-day distribution. The unique geographical setting of the Tibetan Plateau makes it a key region for investigating the interplay between ore deposits, tectonic deformation, and geomorphic evolution in an intraplate setting. In particular, its abundant tectonic and magmatic events have facilitated the formation of numerous porphyry deposits. This study combines big datasets of whole-rock geochemistry, zircon geochronology, and low-temperature thermochronology to reconstruct the tectonic and magmatic evolution, paleoelevation variations, and exhumation history of various terranes within the Tibetan Plateau since Jurassic times and explores how these processes relate to the formation and preservation of porphyry deposits. Our results show that the Qiangtang terrane contains a higher and more clustered concentration of porphyry Cu-Au deposits during intervals of decreasing paleoelevation, whereas the Lhasa terrane predominantly hosts porphyry Cu-(Mo) deposits during intervals of increasing paleoelevation. We propose that intense tectonic and magmatic activity is a critical factor in the formation of porphyry deposits, while significant fluctuations in the paleoelevation of terranes also favor their development. Conversely, regions of the Tibetan Plateau with high degrees of exhumation host fewer porphyry system deposits, and their deposits tend to be of smaller tonnage. This indicates that post-formation exhumation plays a critical role in the preservation of porphyry deposits, with intense exhumation likely leading to erosional loss of previously formed deposits. Areas with lower exhumation volumes should therefore be prioritized as key targets for future mineral exploration.

item.title: The Earliest Cretaceous Intra‐Oceanic Subduction Zone in the Neo‐Tethys: Evidence From an <scp>OIB</scp> ‐Like Intrusion in the Western Indus‐Tsangpo Suture Zone, Southwestern Tibet
item.dc:creator: Feng Liu
Qiang Liu
Keqing Zong
item.description: ABSTRACT The Indus‐Tsangpo Suture Zone (ITSZ), a remnant of the Neo‐Tethys in southern Tibet, has been extensively investigated. OIB‐like (Ocean Island Basalt) rocks are widespread in the ITSZ, yet their origin and geodynamic implications remain controversial. This study identified a mafic intrusion in the western ITSZ. Zircon U–Pb dating yielded ages from 142 to 134 Ma, with ε Hf (t) values (−0.7 to +4.5 and −1.9 to +4.3). The alkaline diabases are light rare earth element (LREE)‐enriched, with low ε Nd (t), high initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr and radiogenic Pb isotopic ratios, consistent with OIB‐like affinity. These features indicate that such short‐lived magmatism originated from two mantle sources: (1) partial melting of the metasomatized mantle wedge induced by slab‐derived melts, and (2) subsequent asthenosphere upwelling resulting from slab breakoff. Together with the intrusion ages and tectonic setting, these data demonstrate that an intra‐oceanic subduction zone existed within the Neo‐Tethys since at least the earliest Cretaceous. Published: 2026-03-08T09:00:26+00:00 Venue: Terra Nova Score: 0.630 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Feng Liu; Qiang Liu; Keqing Zong

Journal: Terra Nova

DOI: 10.1111/ter.70032

Relevance: 0.630 (must_read)

ABSTRACT The Indus‐Tsangpo Suture Zone (ITSZ), a remnant of the Neo‐Tethys in southern Tibet, has been extensively investigated. OIB‐like (Ocean Island Basalt) rocks are widespread in the ITSZ, yet their origin and geodynamic implications remain controversial. This study identified a mafic intrusion in the western ITSZ. Zircon U–Pb dating yielded ages from 142 to 134 Ma, with ε Hf (t) values (−0.7 to +4.5 and −1.9 to +4.3). The alkaline diabases are light rare earth element (LREE)‐enriched, with low ε Nd (t), high initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr and radiogenic Pb isotopic ratios, consistent with OIB‐like affinity. These features indicate that such short‐lived magmatism originated from two mantle sources: (1) partial melting of the metasomatized mantle wedge induced by slab‐derived melts, and (2) subsequent asthenosphere upwelling resulting from slab breakoff. Together with the intrusion ages and tectonic setting, these data demonstrate that an intra‐oceanic subduction zone existed within the Neo‐Tethys since at least the earliest Cretaceous.

item.title: Dual slab stagnation depths controlled by grain-size-induced sporadic low-viscosity zones at around 1000 km depth
item.dc:creator: Jing Li
Keqing Li
Jiangtao Li
Hongzhan Fei
Jiashun Hu
item.description: Seismic tomography reveals widespread stagnant slabs, mainly at depths of ~ 660 and ~ 1000 km, yet a unified explanation for both depths remains lacking. Here we use numerical models that self-consistently track grain-size evolution to investigate controls on slab stagnation depth. Our models show that fossil slabs can induce mantle downwelling that crosses the 660-km discontinuity, where phase-transition-induced grain-size reduction generates a localized low-viscosity zone (LVZ) between 660 and 1000 km depths. Under slow trench retreat and in the presence of an LVZ, slabs penetrate the 660 km discontinuity and stagnate near ~ 1000 km depth. Conversely, under fast trench retreat or without an LVZ, slabs stagnate near ~ 660 km depth or penetrate into the deep mantle. These results highlight sporadic LVZs as a mechanism for the two stagnation depths and imply laterally inhomogeneous mid-mantle viscosity structure driven by sinking fossil slabs from past subduction. Numerical models with evolving grain size suggest a laterally inhomogeneous viscosity layer below 660 km depth, with sporadic low viscosity zones induced by fossil slabs, explaining slab stagnation at around 1000 km depth. Published: 2026-03-03T09:17:24+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.626 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jing Li; Keqing Li; Jiangtao Li; Hongzhan Fei; Jiashun Hu

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-69987-9

Relevance: 0.626 (must_read)

Seismic tomography reveals widespread stagnant slabs, mainly at depths of ~ 660 and ~ 1000 km, yet a unified explanation for both depths remains lacking. Here we use numerical models that self-consistently track grain-size evolution to investigate controls on slab stagnation depth. Our models show that fossil slabs can induce mantle downwelling that crosses the 660-km discontinuity, where phase-transition-induced grain-size reduction generates a localized low-viscosity zone (LVZ) between 660 and 1000 km depths. Under slow trench retreat and in the presence of an LVZ, slabs penetrate the 660 km discontinuity and stagnate near ~ 1000 km depth. Conversely, under fast trench retreat or without an LVZ, slabs stagnate near ~ 660 km depth or penetrate into the deep mantle. These results highlight sporadic LVZs as a mechanism for the two stagnation depths and imply laterally inhomogeneous mid-mantle viscosity structure driven by sinking fossil slabs from past subduction. Numerical models with evolving grain size suggest a laterally inhomogeneous viscosity layer below 660 km depth, with sporadic low viscosity zones induced by fossil slabs, explaining slab stagnation at around 1000 km depth.

item.title: Magma degassing controlling the formation of porphyry copper deposits: A dynamic analysis through numerical modeling
item.dc:creator: Fan Xiao
Changhua Xie
Pingping Zhu
Qiuming Cheng
item.description: Porphyry copper deposits (PCDs) are a major global source of copper and molybdenum and also contain significant reserves of precious metals such as gold, silver, tin, and tungsten. Nevertheless, the role of felsic magma degassing in the formation of porphyry copper mineralization, remains poorly understood. By integrating ore genesis models with physicochemical processes, numerical modeling provides valuable insights into the geodynamics of complex mineralization systems, including PCDs, and facilitates the investigation of key ore-controlling factors. Therefore, this study employs the numerical simulation to analyze the impact of magmatic temperature on the release of ore-forming fluids and the subsequent metal enrichment resulting from magma degassing during PCD formation. A dynamic numerical model, coupling heat transfer, fluid flow, and chemical reaction processes, was developed to conduct experiments characterizing the porphyry cupola, ore shell, and alteration halo under varying degassing temperatures. The results indicate that a magma temperature around 850 °C is most favorable for the formation of high-grade ore shells. This is because excessively low or high magma temperatures impede fluid migration in the surrounding rocks, leading to the development of deep-seated porphyry cupolas and low metal endowments. This study provides an in-depth understanding of the ore-forming dynamics associated with fluid release from degassing magma during PCD formation, examining both fluid migration within the surrounding rocks and the enrichment of ore-forming metals—processes that are highly sensitive to magma temperature. Published: 2026-03-03T07:59:29+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.625 (must_read)

Porphyry copper deposits (PCDs) are a major global source of copper and molybdenum and also contain significant reserves of precious metals such as gold, silver, tin, and tungsten. Nevertheless, the role of felsic magma degassing in the formation of porphyry copper mineralization, remains poorly understood. item.title: Iron mobility and redox transfer in the oceanic lithosphere during nascent subduction of Neotethys Ocean
item.dc:creator: Jia-Le Xu
Yi-Xiang Chen
Chuanzhou Liu
Fang Huang
Sergei Skuzovatov
Tatsuki Tsujimori
Kun Chen
Hans-Peter Schertl
Carlos J. Garrido
item.description: Hydration of oceanic crust and mantle peridotite is a critical process for fluid-rock interaction and element exchange within the oceanic lithosphere. Iron, a key redox-sensitive element, is an important tracer for investigating the redox transfer during this process. However, the mobility of iron and its isotopic composition in serpentinite-related fluids remain poorly known. Rodingites, generally formed through the metasomatic replacement of gabbro by Ca-rich fluids, are typically associated with serpentinites. They provide an excellent medium for studying the composition of serpentinizing and deserpentinization fluids and associated redox transfer. In this study, we report the Fe 3+ /ΣFe ratios, S contents and Fe isotopic compositions of rodingites and gabbros from the Xigaze ophiolite (southern Tibet), in order to assess Fe mobility and redox transfer during oceanic Ca-metasomatism. Based on mineralogical features and formation temperatures, we classified the rodingites into three types: prehnite rodingites (type I, prehnite-rich and garnet-absent, 3+ /ΣFe ratio of rodingites (0.19–0.85) is higher than that of gabbro protoliths (0.17–0.22). However, the δ 56 Fe value of rodingites (−0.19‰–0.12‰) is lower than that of gabbros (0.05‰–0.12‰, in the range of MORB). Isocon analysis of Fe content reveals that prehnite rodingites exhibit only a slight decrease in Fe content. Such rodingites have slightly higher Fe 3+ /ΣFe ratios but lower δ 56 Fe values than the gabbro protolith. These observations suggest that early serpentinizing fluids contain little Fe at relatively reducing conditions. In contrast, garnet rodingites, which formed at elevated P–T conditions, exhibit significantly higher Fe 3+ /ΣFe ratios and S contents but much lower δ 56 Fe values than the gabbro protolith. These results suggest that fluids with light Fe isotopes were released from serpentinite dehydration, most likely in the form of Fe 2+ –SO x complexes, which play a crucial role in the high P–T rodingitization. Compared with prehnite and prehnite–garnet rodingites, such fluids increased the Fe 3+ /ΣFe ratios but decreased the δ 56 Fe values during the formation of garnet rodingite. This study provides important insights into the Fe mobility and redox transfer in the oceanic lithosphere during nascent subduction of Neotethys Ocean, which has great bearing on the redox heterogeneity in subduction zones Published: 2026-03-09T16:41:05+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.617 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jia-Le Xu; Yi-Xiang Chen; Chuanzhou Liu; Fang Huang; Sergei Skuzovatov; Tatsuki Tsujimori; Kun Chen; Hans-Peter Schertl; Carlos J. Garrido

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.03.007

Relevance: 0.617 (must_read)

Hydration of oceanic crust and mantle peridotite is a critical process for fluid-rock interaction and element exchange within the oceanic lithosphere. Iron, a key redox-sensitive element, is an important tracer for investigating the redox transfer during this process. However, the mobility of iron and its isotopic composition in serpentinite-related fluids remain poorly known. Rodingites, generally formed through the metasomatic replacement of gabbro by Ca-rich fluids, are typically associated with serpentinites. They provide an excellent medium for studying the composition of serpentinizing and deserpentinization fluids and associated redox transfer. In this study, we report the Fe 3+ /ΣFe ratios, S contents and Fe isotopic compositions of rodingites and gabbros from the Xigaze ophiolite (southern Tibet), in order to assess Fe mobility and redox transfer during oceanic Ca-metasomatism. Based on mineralogical features and formation temperatures, we classified the rodingites into three types: prehnite rodingites (type I, prehnite-rich and garnet-absent, 3+ /ΣFe ratio of rodingites (0.19–0.85) is higher than that of gabbro protoliths (0.17–0.22). However, the δ 56 Fe value of rodingites (−0.19‰–0.12‰) is lower than that of gabbros (0.05‰–0.12‰, in the range of MORB). Isocon analysis of Fe content reveals that prehnite rodingites exhibit only a slight decrease in Fe content. Such rodingites have slightly higher Fe 3+ /ΣFe ratios but lower δ 56 Fe values than the gabbro protolith. These observations suggest that early serpentinizing fluids contain little Fe at relatively reducing conditions. In contrast, garnet rodingites, which formed at elevated P–T conditions, exhibit significantly higher Fe 3+ /ΣFe ratios and S contents but much lower δ 56 Fe values than the gabbro protolith. These results suggest that fluids with light Fe isotopes were released from serpentinite dehydration, most likely in the form of Fe 2+ –SO x complexes, which play a crucial role in the high P–T rodingitization. Compared with prehnite and prehnite–garnet rodingites, such fluids increased the Fe 3+ /ΣFe ratios but decreased the δ 56 Fe values during the formation of garnet rodingite. This study provides important insights into the Fe mobility and redox transfer in the oceanic lithosphere during nascent subduction of Neotethys Ocean, which has great bearing on the redox heterogeneity in subduction zones

item.title: Mid-Mesoproterozoic mafic magmatism (ca. 1.38 Ga) in the northern Yangtze Craton, China: A geological response to the Columbia supercontinent breakup
item.dc:creator: Yu-Ting Lu
Shuang-Qing Li
Xiao-Feng La
Christian A. Bergemann
Yong-Hong Shi
item.description: Mid-Mesoproterozoic rifting and magmatic events associated with the breakup of the Columbia supercontinent—particularly along the northern margin of the Yangtze Craton—remain poorly studied. This study presents new results from detailed geological mapping, petrography, U-Pb zircon geochronology, whole-rock Sr-Nd-Pb and zircon Hf isotope analyses, and geochemical investigations of newly identified mid-Mesoproterozoic garnet-plagioclase amphibolites in the Susong complex, part of the Dabie Orogenic Belt in central China. U-Pb zircon dating yields ages of ca. 1.38 Ga, and zircon morphology together with geochemical indicators (e.g., Th/U ratios) confirm a magmatic origin. The meta-mafic rocks show high Zr/Hf and low Th/Nb ratios, consistent with a within-plate tectonic setting, and display affinities with ocean-island basalts. Their εNd(t) values (−6 to +7) and zircon εHf(t) values (−13.5 to +7.4) are widely scattered, and one-stage TDM ages for both Nd and Hf cluster at ca. 1.3 Ga, indicating that melt extraction and source differentiation were broadly similar in age. The presence of older model ages (ca. 2.5−2.0 Ga) further reflects the incorporation of ancient crustal or lithospheric fragments into an enriched mantle domain modified by a plume or asthenospheric upwelling. This newly recognized mafic magmatism, together with previously documented coeval felsic magmatism, records a bimodal signature along the Yangtze Craton margin during this period. The results provide new constraints on intracontinental rifting processes and mantle dynamics during the final breakup of the Columbia supercontinent and refine the geodynamic framework of the Mesoproterozoic evolution of the Yangtze Craton. Published: 2026-03-05T20:18:50+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.614 (consider)

Authors: Yu-Ting Lu; Shuang-Qing Li; Xiao-Feng La; Christian A. Bergemann; Yong-Hong Shi

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

Relevance: 0.614 (consider)

Mid-Mesoproterozoic rifting and magmatic events associated with the breakup of the Columbia supercontinent—particularly along the northern margin of the Yangtze Craton—remain poorly studied. This study presents new results from detailed geological mapping, petrography, U-Pb zircon geochronology, whole-rock Sr-Nd-Pb and zircon Hf isotope analyses, and geochemical investigations of newly identified mid-Mesoproterozoic garnet-plagioclase amphibolites in the Susong complex, part of the Dabie Orogenic Belt in central China. U-Pb zircon dating yields ages of ca. 1.38 Ga, and zircon morphology together with geochemical indicators (e.g., Th/U ratios) confirm a magmatic origin. The meta-mafic rocks show high Zr/Hf and low Th/Nb ratios, consistent with a within-plate tectonic setting, and display affinities with ocean-island basalts. Their εNd(t) values (−6 to +7) and zircon εHf(t) values (−13.5 to +7.4) are widely scattered, and one-stage TDM ages for both Nd and Hf cluster at ca. 1.3 Ga, indicating that melt extraction and source differentiation were broadly similar in age. The presence of older model ages (ca. 2.5−2.0 Ga) further reflects the incorporation of ancient crustal or lithospheric fragments into an enriched mantle domain modified by a plume or asthenospheric upwelling. This newly recognized mafic magmatism, together with previously documented coeval felsic magmatism, records a bimodal signature along the Yangtze Craton margin during this period. The results provide new constraints on intracontinental rifting processes and mantle dynamics during the final breakup of the Columbia supercontinent and refine the geodynamic framework of the Mesoproterozoic evolution of the Yangtze Craton.

item.title: Fluid transport and reaction during disseminated mineralization in orogenic gold systems
item.dc:creator: Peng-Cong Zhang
Hao-Cheng Yu
Luca Smeraglia
Yong-Bao Gao
Chao Li
Jia-Nan Fu
Zhao-Hua Bai
Rui Wang
Kun-Feng Qiu
item.description: Disseminated mineralization is responsible for much of the production from orogenic gold deposits, and deciphering the nature of reactive fluid flow across the macro- to microscopic scale is crucial for understanding the mineralization processes. We integrated structural analysis, microstructural observations, whole-rock geochemistry, and thermodynamic modeling at the Liba orogenic gold deposit in the West Qinling orogen, central China, aiming to unravel the behavior of fluid in slate-hosted disseminated mineralization. The presence of deformed slaty foliation and hydrothermal sericite S-C fabrics indicates that the east-west−striking orebodies are hosted within sinistral brittle-ductile shear zones, which act as deposit-scale fluid migration conduits. The spatial distribution of pyrite within the mineralized slates closely aligns with pore networks and grain boundaries, suggesting that grain-scale enhanced permeability and fluid flow during mineralization were primarily accommodated by microcracking along grain boundaries. Combined with whole-rock geochemical data and thermodynamic modeling, we reveal that under conditions where rock permeability is sufficient for fluid transport, gold mineralization efficiency depends on the degree of reaction between the reactive fluid and Fe-bearing minerals. Compared to siliceous slate, the higher abundance of Fe-bearing minerals in argillaceous slate enhances reactivity at the fluid-mineral interface, promoting sulfide formation and gold precipitation. This study demonstrates that the physicochemical interplay between structural deformation and geochemical reactivity fundamentally controls disseminated mineralization in slate-hosted orogenic gold systems. Published: 2026-03-09T18:23:49+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.613 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Peng-Cong Zhang; Hao-Cheng Yu; Luca Smeraglia; Yong-Bao Gao; Chao Li; Jia-Nan Fu; Zhao-Hua Bai; Rui Wang; Kun-Feng Qiu

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38735.1

Relevance: 0.613 (consider)

Disseminated mineralization is responsible for much of the production from orogenic gold deposits, and deciphering the nature of reactive fluid flow across the macro- to microscopic scale is crucial for understanding the mineralization processes. We integrated structural analysis, microstructural observations, whole-rock geochemistry, and thermodynamic modeling at the Liba orogenic gold deposit in the West Qinling orogen, central China, aiming to unravel the behavior of fluid in slate-hosted disseminated mineralization. The presence of deformed slaty foliation and hydrothermal sericite S-C fabrics indicates that the east-west−striking orebodies are hosted within sinistral brittle-ductile shear zones, which act as deposit-scale fluid migration conduits. The spatial distribution of pyrite within the mineralized slates closely aligns with pore networks and grain boundaries, suggesting that grain-scale enhanced permeability and fluid flow during mineralization were primarily accommodated by microcracking along grain boundaries. Combined with whole-rock geochemical data and thermodynamic modeling, we reveal that under conditions where rock permeability is sufficient for fluid transport, gold mineralization efficiency depends on the degree of reaction between the reactive fluid and Fe-bearing minerals. Compared to siliceous slate, the higher abundance of Fe-bearing minerals in argillaceous slate enhances reactivity at the fluid-mineral interface, promoting sulfide formation and gold precipitation. This study demonstrates that the physicochemical interplay between structural deformation and geochemical reactivity fundamentally controls disseminated mineralization in slate-hosted orogenic gold systems.

item.title: High-titanium mare basalts: From petrogenesis to lunar evolution
item.dc:creator: Haojie Chen
Bin Su
Yi Chen
Sheng Gou
Hengci Tian
Fu-Yuan Wu
item.description: High-Ti mare basalts, characterized by their unique titanium-rich compositions (TiO2 contents up to 16 wt.%), provide critical insights into lunar magma ocean (LMO) differentiation, the structure and composition of the lunar mantle, and lunar internal dynamics. Here we systematically synthesize the spatiotemporal distribution, petrology, mineralogy, and geochemistry of high-Ti mare basalts. By integrating these compiled data with thermodynamic modeling, we present a comprehensive evaluation of the four prevailing petrogenetic models: (1) ilmenite-bearing cumulate (IBC) remelting, (2) IBC assimilation, (3) reaction of IBC-derived melts with early LMO cumulates, and (4) mantle source hybridization. Our analyses confirm that the distinct compositional characteristics of high-Ti mare basalts were inherited from late-stage, ilmenite-bearing LMO cumulates. However, we find that the IBC direct remelting, assimilation, and melt-rock reaction models (Models 1–3) each face significant limitations, failing to reconcile observed cumulate components, reproduce the full spectrum of melt compositions, or satisfy thermodynamic viability. In contrast, the mantle hybridization model (Model 4) emerges as the most promising mechanism, successfully satisfying multiple experimental and observational constraints for lunar high-Ti magmatism. We suggest that high-Ti mare basalts are not simple melting products of a single cumulate source. Instead, they represent the combined products of lunar mantle overturn, cumulate hybridization, and partial melting during the Moon’s long-term evolution. This scenario underscores a lunar interior with a far more complex physicochemical state than traditionally envisioned. Future research should prioritize constraining high-Ti mare basalts’ specific source composition and depth of origin, the mechanisms driving their distinct two-stage eruptive history (3.5–3.9 Ga and <2.0 Ga), and their spatial distribution patterns. Resolving these questions is paramount for understanding LMO differentiation, mantle overturn, the Moon’s thermal evolution, and the origin of the lunar hemispheric dichotomy. Published: 2026-03-09T07:53:29+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.608 (consider)

High-Ti mare basalts, characterized by their unique titanium-rich compositions (TiO2 contents up to 16 wt.%), provide critical insights into lunar magma ocean (LMO) differentiation, the structure and composition of the lunar mantle, and lunar internal dynamics. Here we systematically synthesize the spatiotemporal distribution, petrology, mineralogy, and geochemistry of high-Ti mare basalts. By integrating these compiled data with thermodynamic modeling, we present a comprehensive evaluation of the four prevailing petrogenetic models: (1) ilmenite-bearing cumulate (IBC) remelting, (2) IBC assimilation, (3) reaction of IBC-derived melts with early LMO cumulates, and (4) mantle source hybridization. Our analyses confirm that the distinct compositional characteristics of high-Ti mare basalts were inherited from late-stage, ilmenite-bearing LMO cumulates. However, we find that the IBC direct remelting, assimilation, and melt-rock reaction models (Models 1–3) each face significant limitations, failing to reconcile observed cumulate components, reproduce the full spectrum of melt compositions, or satisfy thermodynamic viability. In contrast, the mantle hybridization model (Model 4) emerges as the most promising mechanism, successfully satisfying multiple experimental and observational constraints for lunar high-Ti magmatism. We suggest that high-Ti mare basalts are not simple melting products of a single cumulate source. Instead, they represent the combined products of lunar mantle overturn, cumulate hybridization, and partial melting during the Moon’s long-term evolution. This scenario underscores a lunar interior with a far more complex physicochemical state than traditionally envisioned. Future research should prioritize constraining high-Ti mare basalts’ specific source composition and depth of origin, the mechanisms driving their distinct two-stage eruptive history (3.5–3.9 Ga and <2.0 Ga), and their spatial distribution patterns. Resolving these questions is paramount for understanding LMO differentiation, mantle overturn, the Moon’s thermal evolution, and the origin of the lunar hemispheric dichotomy.

item.title: A common mantle source for strongly silica-undersaturated magmas: Insights from Zn-Fe isotopes in kimberlites
item.dc:creator: Ronghua Cai
Andrea Giuliani
Paolo A. Sossi
Tian-Hao Wu
Yongsheng He
D. Graham Pearson
Stephen F. Foley
Jingao Liu
item.description: Kimberlites contain distinct asthenospheric and lithospheric mantle components. Disentangling their relative contributions is a challenging yet important goal to constrain the kimberlite sources. We analyzed the Zn-Fe isotope compositions of 55 globally distributed kimberlites. The Zn-Fe isotopes of kimberlites define a strong positive correlation from isotopically light compositions akin to cratonic peridotites to heavy compositions that are similar to those of melilitites and nephelinites globally. This covariation can be explained by mixing between cratonic mantle peridotites and isotopically similar uncontaminated kimberlite melts, with the observed heavy Zn-Fe isotopic signatures potentially originating from low-degree partial melting of asthenospheric sources. The similarities of trace elements and isotopes between uncontaminated kimberlite melts, nephelinites and melilitites reveal that these strongly silica-undersaturated magmas tap similar mantle sources in the convective mantle. Partial melting of this common source under different melting conditions can produce the spectrum of mantle melts from kimberlitic to silica-undersaturated basaltic melts without necessarily invoking compositional variations in the mantle source. Published: 2026-03-06T14:49:23+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.604 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ronghua Cai; Andrea Giuliani; Paolo A. Sossi; Tian-Hao Wu; Yongsheng He; D. Graham Pearson; Stephen F. Foley; Jingao Liu

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.119949

Relevance: 0.604 (consider)

Kimberlites contain distinct asthenospheric and lithospheric mantle components. Disentangling their relative contributions is a challenging yet important goal to constrain the kimberlite sources. We analyzed the Zn-Fe isotope compositions of 55 globally distributed kimberlites. The Zn-Fe isotopes of kimberlites define a strong positive correlation from isotopically light compositions akin to cratonic peridotites to heavy compositions that are similar to those of melilitites and nephelinites globally. This covariation can be explained by mixing between cratonic mantle peridotites and isotopically similar uncontaminated kimberlite melts, with the observed heavy Zn-Fe isotopic signatures potentially originating from low-degree partial melting of asthenospheric sources. The similarities of trace elements and isotopes between uncontaminated kimberlite melts, nephelinites and melilitites reveal that these strongly silica-undersaturated magmas tap similar mantle sources in the convective mantle. Partial melting of this common source under different melting conditions can produce the spectrum of mantle melts from kimberlitic to silica-undersaturated basaltic melts without necessarily invoking compositional variations in the mantle source.

item.title: Diffusive copper redistribution from shallow degassed magmas to mafic enclaves. Insight from anomalously Cu-enriched enclaves from Bezymianny volcano, Kamchatka
item.dc:creator: Vesta O. Davydova
Vasily D. Shcherbakov
Pavel Yu. Plechov
Vasily O. Yapaskurt
Yuri D. Shcherbakov
Alexander B. Perepelov
Nikolay V. Brianskii
Viktor S. Antipin
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research
item.description: Mafic enclaves enriched in sulfides and copper compared to their host magmas are found in felsic plutons associated with porphyry deposits and in eruptive products of arc volcanoes. The origin of these Cu-rich enclaves is usually linked to either intrinsically Cu-rich parental magmas or to extensive fractionation of primitive magmas under sulfide-undersaturated conditions before they are stored in shallow felsic reservoirs. Using Bezymianny volcano as a case study, we suggest an alternative mechanism for the formation of Cu-rich mafic enclaves. Detailed petrographic analysis shows that sulfides in Bezymianny enclaves evolve from scarce early Cu-poor MSS (chilled monosulfide solid solution close to pyrrhotite or euhedral pyrrhotite with thin ISS rims) enclosed in phenocrysts, to Cu-rich ISS (intermediate solid solution close to cubanite) in matrix glass and vesicles. This increase in Cu content with crystallization requires the external addition of Cu. Mineral oxybarometry indicates that the shallow-stored magma (<1 kbar) had an oxygen fugacity of 0 to +1 log units above NNO. Intense degassing of S-bearing aqueous fluid kept sulfur concentrations in silicate melts below sulfide saturation, destabilizing sulfides and causing incompatible behavior of chalcophile elements. Consequently, Cu accumulated in silicate melt until inputs of sulfide-bearing mafic magma from deeper levels (9–2 kbar) triggered its diffusive redistribution into enclaves. Cu diffused from the Cu-rich, sulfide-free silicate melt of the shallow reservoir into the Cu-poor silicate melt of mafic enclaves, and subsequently into their sulfide phases. High diffusion rates of monovalent Cu allow small enclaves to achieve full Cu enrichment within several weeks. Published: 2026-03-09T16:47:29+00:00 Venue: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research Score: 0.603 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vesta O. Davydova; Vasily D. Shcherbakov; Pavel Yu. Plechov; Vasily O. Yapaskurt; Yuri D. Shcherbakov; Alexander B. Perepelov; Nikolay V. Brianskii; Viktor S. Antipin

Journal: Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.jvolgeores.2026.108588

Relevance: 0.603 (consider)

Mafic enclaves enriched in sulfides and copper compared to their host magmas are found in felsic plutons associated with porphyry deposits and in eruptive products of arc volcanoes. The origin of these Cu-rich enclaves is usually linked to either intrinsically Cu-rich parental magmas or to extensive fractionation of primitive magmas under sulfide-undersaturated conditions before they are stored in shallow felsic reservoirs. Using Bezymianny volcano as a case study, we suggest an alternative mechanism for the formation of Cu-rich mafic enclaves. Detailed petrographic analysis shows that sulfides in Bezymianny enclaves evolve from scarce early Cu-poor MSS (chilled monosulfide solid solution close to pyrrhotite or euhedral pyrrhotite with thin ISS rims) enclosed in phenocrysts, to Cu-rich ISS (intermediate solid solution close to cubanite) in matrix glass and vesicles. This increase in Cu content with crystallization requires the external addition of Cu. Mineral oxybarometry indicates that the shallow-stored magma (<1 kbar) had an oxygen fugacity of 0 to +1 log units above NNO. Intense degassing of S-bearing aqueous fluid kept sulfur concentrations in silicate melts below sulfide saturation, destabilizing sulfides and causing incompatible behavior of chalcophile elements. Consequently, Cu accumulated in silicate melt until inputs of sulfide-bearing mafic magma from deeper levels (9–2 kbar) triggered its diffusive redistribution into enclaves. Cu diffused from the Cu-rich, sulfide-free silicate melt of the shallow reservoir into the Cu-poor silicate melt of mafic enclaves, and subsequently into their sulfide phases. High diffusion rates of monovalent Cu allow small enclaves to achieve full Cu enrichment within several weeks.

item.title: Blueschist Dislocation Creep and Glide in Subduction Systems: Constraints From Glaucophane Experiments
item.dc:creator: Lonnie J. Hufford
Leif Tokle
Whitney M. Behr
Luiz F. G. Morales
Claudio Madonna
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Blueschists are a defining component of subducting oceanic crust and influence slab and interface rheology across thermal regimes. Glaucophane, the dominant sodic amphibole in blueschist, is a key rheology‐controlling phase, yet its dislocation‐based strength is poorly constrained. We conducted load‐stepping experiments in a Griggs apparatus at 600–700C and 1.0 GPa over shear strain rates from ∼ to 1.2, using a starting grain size of n = 3, Q = 450 15 kJ/mol, A = 2.32 × and dislocation glide A = , = 22 GPa, H = 800 kJ/mol, p = 1, and q = 2. Extrapolation to geologic conditions suggests that dislocation glide will not operate under steady‐state subduction‐interface conditions, and is likely only relevant during transient high stress perturbations. Dislocation creep dominates at temperatures above 450°C at grain sizes of ∼1 mm or larger. Comparison with published quartz/metasediment and eclogite flow laws indicates that blueschist strengths are intermediate between these endmembers, implying a meaningful contribution to long‐term interface strength in mafic‐dominated (sediment‐poor) margins. Together, these results refine glaucophane‐controlled blueschist rheology and its implications for subduction zone mechanics. Published: 2026-03-09T11:24:21+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.601 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lonnie J. Hufford; Leif Tokle; Whitney M. Behr; Luiz F. G. Morales; Claudio Madonna

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2025jb033622

Relevance: 0.601 (consider)

Abstract Blueschists are a defining component of subducting oceanic crust and influence slab and interface rheology across thermal regimes. Glaucophane, the dominant sodic amphibole in blueschist, is a key rheology‐controlling phase, yet its dislocation‐based strength is poorly constrained. We conducted load‐stepping experiments in a Griggs apparatus at 600–700C and 1.0 GPa over shear strain rates from ∼ to 1.2, using a starting grain size of n = 3, Q = 450 15 kJ/mol, A = 2.32 × and dislocation glide A = , = 22 GPa, H = 800 kJ/mol, p = 1, and q = 2. Extrapolation to geologic conditions suggests that dislocation glide will not operate under steady‐state subduction‐interface conditions, and is likely only relevant during transient high stress perturbations. Dislocation creep dominates at temperatures above 450°C at grain sizes of ∼1 mm or larger. Comparison with published quartz/metasediment and eclogite flow laws indicates that blueschist strengths are intermediate between these endmembers, implying a meaningful contribution to long‐term interface strength in mafic‐dominated (sediment‐poor) margins. Together, these results refine glaucophane‐controlled blueschist rheology and its implications for subduction zone mechanics.

item.title: Magmatic water variation in the Neo-Tethys: Controlled by convergence rate and slab geometry
item.dc:creator: Shaochen Wu
Rui Wang
Xinyi Zhong
item.description: Water content in magmatic systems exerts a pivotal control on processes from melt generation to eruption, yet the magmatic water variation from subduction to collision and its drivers remain enigmatic. The Gangdese belt in southern Tibet, which archives magmatism from Neo-Tethyan oceanic lithosphere subduction to collision, provides a unique temporal record to address this gap. We apply a fractional crystallization model to both major and trace elements of whole-rock geochemistry data in the Gangdese belt, and we use the difference between amphibole and plagioclase proportions to indicate the variation in magmatic water. Combined with numerical simulations, we link the variation in magmatic water of the Neo-Tethys to the change of convergence rate and slab geometry, including normal subduction, slab rollback, and flat-slab subduction. Low convergence rates and slab rollback elevate temperatures, enhancing water release and magmatic hydration, while high convergence rates and flat-slab subduction cool the system, limiting slab dehydration. From 215 Ma to 160 Ma, shifting slab geometry from normal to rollback to flat-slab matched with magmatic water variability, whereas 160−15 Ma variations correlated with convergence rate changes. Using the fractional crystallization model, we obtain the variation in magmatic water of the Neo-Tethys subduction zone. We further explore its connection to the change of subduction parameters, advancing understanding of subduction zone water cycles and their broader implications for arc magmatism. Published: 2026-03-09T18:23:45+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.601 (consider)

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38792.1

Relevance: 0.601 (consider)

Water content in magmatic systems exerts a pivotal control on processes from melt generation to eruption, yet the magmatic water variation from subduction to collision and its drivers remain enigmatic. The Gangdese belt in southern Tibet, which archives magmatism from Neo-Tethyan oceanic lithosphere subduction to collision, provides a unique temporal record to address this gap. We apply a fractional crystallization model to both major and trace elements of whole-rock geochemistry data in the Gangdese belt, and we use the difference between amphibole and plagioclase proportions to indicate the variation in magmatic water. Combined with numerical simulations, we link the variation in magmatic water of the Neo-Tethys to the change of convergence rate and slab geometry, including normal subduction, slab rollback, and flat-slab subduction. Low convergence rates and slab rollback elevate temperatures, enhancing water release and magmatic hydration, while high convergence rates and flat-slab subduction cool the system, limiting slab dehydration. From 215 Ma to 160 Ma, shifting slab geometry from normal to rollback to flat-slab matched with magmatic water variability, whereas 160−15 Ma variations correlated with convergence rate changes. Using the fractional crystallization model, we obtain the variation in magmatic water of the Neo-Tethys subduction zone. We further explore its connection to the change of subduction parameters, advancing understanding of subduction zone water cycles and their broader implications for arc magmatism.

item.title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
item.dc:creator: Han Wu
Wenxin Xie
Satish C. Singh
Hélène Carton
Graham M. Kent
Adrien F. Arnulf
Alistair J. Harding
item.description: Abstract Magmatically-accreted upper crust along mid-ocean ridges is traditionally considered to consist of lava flows overlying a sheeted-dyke complex. However, how the upper crust is formed at hotspot-influenced ridge segments remains unknown. Using three-dimensional seismic reflection data, here we report images of lava flow layering beneath Axial volcano on the Juan de Fuca Ridge. These lava flow layers dip inward towards the caldera and rift zones, resulting from subsidence caused by magma withdrawal beneath the summit and rift zone extension. Furthermore, our images reveal that the classic, laterally continuous sheeted-dyke complex is absent. Instead, lava flow layers are rotated and brought into direct contact with the magma domain, leading to partial assimilation of these units. Three-dimensional images also show that melt is injected outward along lava flow layers, suggesting that the upper crust at Axial volcano is formed by complex interactions between injected melt sills and lava flow layers. Published: 2026-03-05T15:06:21+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.600 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Han Wu; Wenxin Xie; Satish C. Singh; Hélène Carton; Graham M. Kent; Adrien F. Arnulf; Alistair J. Harding

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x

Relevance: 0.600 (consider)

Abstract Magmatically-accreted upper crust along mid-ocean ridges is traditionally considered to consist of lava flows overlying a sheeted-dyke complex. However, how the upper crust is formed at hotspot-influenced ridge segments remains unknown. Using three-dimensional seismic reflection data, here we report images of lava flow layering beneath Axial volcano on the Juan de Fuca Ridge. These lava flow layers dip inward towards the caldera and rift zones, resulting from subsidence caused by magma withdrawal beneath the summit and rift zone extension. Furthermore, our images reveal that the classic, laterally continuous sheeted-dyke complex is absent. Instead, lava flow layers are rotated and brought into direct contact with the magma domain, leading to partial assimilation of these units. Three-dimensional images also show that melt is injected outward along lava flow layers, suggesting that the upper crust at Axial volcano is formed by complex interactions between injected melt sills and lava flow layers.

item.title: Petrogenesis, metallogenesis, and tectonic setting of W-fertile granitoids in the Eastern Tianshan, NW China — geochronological, geochemical, and Sr Nd isotope constraints
item.dc:creator: Yanxing Li
Xiaohua Deng
Xiheng He
Matthew J. Brzozowski
Jingguo Du
Xiangyu Wu
item.description: The Eastern Tianshan (ET), a key metallogenic belt within the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB), hosts extensive W–polymetallic mineralization. Numerous W-related intrusions are distributed in the Central Tianshan Terrane (CTT) and the Kumishi area; however, the controls on W fertility—including magma source characteristics, tectonic setting, and crustal evolution—remain contentious. This study integrates zircon U Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and Sr Nd isotopic data from barren and W-fertile granites to constrain their petrogenesis and metallogenic mechanisms. Zircon U Pb ages indicate that W-fertile intrusions in the CTT were emplaced at 242–231 Ma, whereas those in Kumishi formed earlier at 312–293 Ma, both during collisional orogenesis. CTT granitoids (e.g., Xiaobaishitou and Shadong) are predominantly I-type, characterized by low A/CNK ratios (0.96–1.02), moderately radiogenic ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr) i values (0.7067–0.7142), slightly negative ε Nd (t) values (−3.10 to −1.3), and Mesoproterozoic T DM2 model ages. In contrast, Kumishi granitoids include both S-type (A/CNK = 1.05–1.48) and I-type (A/CNK = 0.98–1.06) varieties, with more negative ε Nd (t) values (−7.0 to −2.83) and older T DM2 ages (1304–1632 Ma), indicating stronger involvement of Precambrian crustal components. All W-fertile granites are enriched in LREEs and depleted in HREEs, Ba, Sr, Nb, P, Eu, and Ti, consistent with advanced magmatic differentiation. Kumishi W-fertile intrusions display more negative ε Nd (t), older model ages, and lower Zr/Hf and Nb/Ta ratios, suggesting derivation from W-enriched crustal sources with minor mantle input followed by extensive fractional crystallization. In contrast, W-fertile and barren intrusions in the CTT exhibit broadly similar isotopic and elemental characteristics, implying comparable magma sources. These results define two contrasting metallogenic pathways. In Kumishi, W mineralization is primarily controlled by source inheritance from W-rich crust combined with strong magmatic differentiation. In the CTT, W enrichment is largely independent of source composition and instead governed by tungsten partitioning during the magmatic–hydrothermal transition and subsequent fluid–rock interaction. Collectively, these findings provide new constraints on tungsten enrichment in collisional orogenic systems and offer a refined framework for regional W exploration in the ET. Published: 2026-03-03T07:59:22+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.598 (consider)

Relevance: 0.598 (consider)

The Eastern Tianshan (ET), a key metallogenic belt within the Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB), hosts extensive W–polymetallic mineralization. Numerous W-related intrusions are distributed in the Central Tianshan Terrane (CTT) and the Kumishi area; however, the controls on W fertility—including magma source characteristics, tectonic setting, and crustal evolution—remain contentious. In contrast, Kumishi granitoids include both S-type (A/CNK = 1.05–1.48) and I-type (A/CNK = 0.98–1.06) varieties, with more negative ε Nd (t) values (−7.0 to −2.83) and older T DM2 ages (1304–1632 Ma), indicating stronger involvement of Precambrian crustal components. All W-fertile granites are enriched in LREEs and depleted in HREEs, Ba, Sr, Nb, P, Eu, and Ti, consistent with advanced magmatic differentiation. Kumishi W-fertile intrusions display more negative ε Nd (t), older model ages, and lower Zr/Hf and Nb/Ta ratios, suggesting derivation from W-enriched crustal sources with minor mantle input followed by extensive fractional crystallization. In the CTT, W enrichment is largely independent of source composition and instead governed by tungsten partitioning during the magmatic–hydrothermal transition and subsequent fluid–rock interaction. Collectively, these findings provide new constraints on tungsten enrichment in collisional orogenic systems and offer a refined framework for regional W exploration in the ET.

item.title: Ultrahigh-Temperature Metamorphism in Interior Antarctica Reveals Mature Orogeny during Assembly of Gondwana
item.dc:creator: Ting-Nan Gong
Wei-(RZ) Wang
Yue Zhao
Nathan Robert Daczko
Xiaohu Wang
Chunjing Wei
item.description: Abstract Ultrahigh-temperature (UHT) metamorphism, recording extreme crustal thermal regimes, provides pivotal insights into the evolution of ancient orogens. Terranes with UHT metamorphism distributed within the continental fragments of the former Gondwana megacontinent hold critical clues to the processes and dynamics associated with Gondwana’s assembly and evolution. The Kuunga Orogen documents the collision between northern and southern Gondwana, marking the final assembly of the Gondwana megacontinent. However, the evolution of this orogen, especially its eastern section, which is largely covered by the Antarctic ice cap, remains poorly constrained. Based on integrated petrological, phase equilibrium modeling, and petrochronological analyses, we report UHT metamorphism in spinel-bearing pelitic granulites from the glacial moraines in the Grove Mountains, East Antarctica. Phase equilibrium modeling and mineral thermometry reveal peak temperature conditions of ≥900 °C and ~0.7 GPa for pelitic granulites characterized by the assemblage garnet + spinel + K-feldspar + plagioclase + ilmenite ± sillimanite ± rutile ± biotite + melt, with a subsequent near-isobaric cooling path to ~800 °C. Petrochronological analyses of multiple dateable phases were conducted to constrain the timing of the UHT metamorphism. Zircon and monazite U–Pb dating constrain peak UHT metamorphism to predate ~535 Ma, with cooling to the solidus by ~520 Ma. Biotite/K-feldspar Rb-Sr isochron ages of ~456–440 Ma are interpreted to represent the post-peak cooling stage at ~300–400 °C. Combined with existing regional data, our new results define a two-stage cooling history: an initial rapid cooling at ~18 °C/Myr from ~535 to 500 Ma, followed by a significantly slower stage at < 3 °C/Myr from ~500 to 440 Ma. The UHT metamorphism recorded in the moraine samples indicates that extreme thermal perturbations may have facilitated crustal evolution and maturation of the subglacial orogen in East Antarctica. These findings shed new light on the geodynamic evolution of the eastern Kuunga Orogen and the assembly history of Gondwana. Published: 2026-03-05T12:42:34+00:00 Venue: Journal of Petrology Score: 0.598 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ting-Nan Gong; Wei-(RZ) Wang; Yue Zhao; Nathan Robert Daczko; Xiaohu Wang; Chunjing Wei

Journal: Journal of Petrology

Abstract Ultrahigh-temperature (UHT) metamorphism, recording extreme crustal thermal regimes, provides pivotal insights into the evolution of ancient orogens. Terranes with UHT metamorphism distributed within the continental fragments of the former Gondwana megacontinent hold critical clues to the processes and dynamics associated with Gondwana’s assembly and evolution. The Kuunga Orogen documents the collision between northern and southern Gondwana, marking the final assembly of the Gondwana megacontinent. However, the evolution of this orogen, especially its eastern section, which is largely covered by the Antarctic ice cap, remains poorly constrained. Based on integrated petrological, phase equilibrium modeling, and petrochronological analyses, we report UHT metamorphism in spinel-bearing pelitic granulites from the glacial moraines in the Grove Mountains, East Antarctica. Phase equilibrium modeling and mineral thermometry reveal peak temperature conditions of ≥900 °C and ~0.7 GPa for pelitic granulites characterized by the assemblage garnet + spinel + K-feldspar + plagioclase + ilmenite ± sillimanite ± rutile ± biotite + melt, with a subsequent near-isobaric cooling path to ~800 °C. Petrochronological analyses of multiple dateable phases were conducted to constrain the timing of the UHT metamorphism. Zircon and monazite U–Pb dating constrain peak UHT metamorphism to predate ~535 Ma, with cooling to the solidus by ~520 Ma. Biotite/K-feldspar Rb-Sr isochron ages of ~456–440 Ma are interpreted to represent the post-peak cooling stage at ~300–400 °C. Combined with existing regional data, our new results define a two-stage cooling history: an initial rapid cooling at ~18 °C/Myr from ~535 to 500 Ma, followed by a significantly slower stage at < 3 °C/Myr from ~500 to 440 Ma. The UHT metamorphism recorded in the moraine samples indicates that extreme thermal perturbations may have facilitated crustal evolution and maturation of the subglacial orogen in East Antarctica. These findings shed new light on the geodynamic evolution of the eastern Kuunga Orogen and the assembly history of Gondwana.

item.title: Metamorphism and chronology of Neoproterozoic granulite facies metasedimentary rock in northwestern Yangtze Block and its tectonic implications
item.dc:creator: Linfeng Feng
Haozheng Wang
Byung Choon Lee
Jinghao Fu
Yi Zhang
Heng Liu
Meimei Han
Bo Hu
Xiahong Cui
Mingguo Zhai
item.description: The role of the Yangtze Block in the Rodinia Supercontinent is still debated. The garnet-biotite metatexite in the Micangshan area, northwestern margin of the Yangtze Block, mainly consists of garnet, biotite, plagioclase, K-feldspar, and quartz with minor ilmenite and accessory minerals of zircon and monazite. The prograde-stage is inclusions inside the garnet, including quartz, biotite, plagioclase, and monazite. The peak-stage is characterized by equilibrium garnet, biotite, plagioclase, K-feldspar, quartz, and ilmenite. The retrograde-stage represents the anhedral small flaky biotite and the coexistence of minor anhedral plagioclase and K-feldspar. Combining the P-T estimation from the pseudosection by Perple_X, quartz-in-garnet (QuiG) Raman-barometer, and conventional geothermobarometers, the prograde-stage P-T condition is constrained at 477–555°C, 5.0–6.5 kbar, and the peak and retrograde stages were determined as 800–830°C, 7–8.8 kbar and 603–674°C, 4.5–5.5 kbar, respectively. A clockwise P-T path was constructed. Metamorphic zircons in this rock yield metamorphic ages of 808 ± 5 Ma and 728–746 Ma. Zircons formed at ca. 808 Ma revealed a crystallization temperature of 727 ± 12°C, which is lower than the peak-stage temperature. Thus, zircons were regarded as retrograde products. Inherited zircons in this rock yielded 206 Pb/ 238 U apparent dates of 828–939 Ma. These zircons show geochemical characteristics of orogenic or arc-related origins. The 206 Pb/ 238 U dates of the monazite span in the range of 788–822 Ma, which suggests two centres at 817 Ma and 803 Ma. As monazite hosted in the garnet, it could record the metamorphic history from the prograde. Thus, 817 Ma is believed to be the time of peak-stage metamorphism, and the 808–788 Ma is interpreted as retrograde time. Based on all of the above, the northwestern margin of the Yangtze Block is suggested to have experienced the collision process, resulting in granulite facies metamorphism at ca. 817 Ma. This study provides additional constraints to decode the role of the Yangtze Block in the Rodinia evolution. Published: 2026-03-06T05:30:04+00:00 Venue: International Geology Review Score: 0.596 (consider)

A clockwise P-T path was constructed. Metamorphic zircons in this rock yield metamorphic ages of 808 ± 5 Ma and 728–746 Ma. Inherited zircons in this rock yielded 206 Pb/ 238 U apparent dates of 828–939 Ma. These zircons show geochemical characteristics of orogenic or arc-related origins. This study provides additional constraints to decode the role of the Yangtze Block in the Rodinia evolution.

item.title: Genesis of copper-deficient skarn iron deposits in the North China Craton: Insights from apatite and whole-rock geochemistry
item.dc:creator: Jun-Wu Zhang
Lin Li
Ju-Quan Zhang
Fang-Yue Wang
Xian Liang
item.description: The North China Craton in eastern China contains several skarn iron ore clusters that are notably copper-deficient compared to those in the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Belt, but their genesis remains unclear. We collected apatite major element and whole rocks major and trace element data from skarn Fe/Cu deposit-associated and barren intermediate–acid plutons in both regions. A comparison of the whole-rock and apatite geochemistry of plutons with different fertility levels (Barren, Fe-fertile, and Cu-bearing) reveals that mineralized plutons result from multiple coupled factors. We employed a Random Forest model to classify pluton fertility (Cu-bearing, Fe-fertile, and barren) based on apatite-equilibrium melt parameters (melt F, Cl, SO 3 , and ΔFMQ). The model, trained on a dataset of 425 apatite analyses (181 Cu-bearing, 154 Fe-fertile, and 90 barren), achieved robust performance with an accuracy, precision, and recall of 0.88, and a five-fold cross-validation F1 score of 0.87 ± 0.02. This analysis reveals distinct geochemical signatures: Barren plutons have lower contents of F Melt , Cl Melt , and SO 3(Melt) . Fe-fertile plutons show the highest Cl Melt and higher ΔFMQ. Conversely, Cu-bearing plutons have higher SO 3(Melt) but lower Cl Melt and ΔFMQ than Fe-fertile plutons. Using an integrated set of apatite and whole-rock indicators, we established diagnostic thresholds: Barren plutons are distinguished by lower median values of La/Yb ratio (17), AST (apatite saturation temperatures; 845 °C), melt F (1180 ppm) and SO 3 (32 ppm) contents in apatite. Cu-bearing plutons are defined by higher median values of La/Yb ratio (35), ε Nd (t) (−6.93), AST (944 °C), melt F (1819 ppm) and SO 3 (64 ppm) contents, and moderate oxidizing conditions (ΔFMQ −1.8 – +2.9). Fe-fertile plutons are characterized by lower median values of La/Yb ratio (16), ε Nd (t) (−13.31), and AST (865 °C), coupled with higher median values of melt F (1637 ppm), Cl (2366 ppm), and SO 3 (66 ppm) contents, and higher oxidizing conditions (ΔFMQ −0.8 – +4.3). These indicators provide a practical tool for identifying Cu-fertile plutons within the North China Craton. Based on these findings, comparative analysis suggests that the copper deficiency in intrusions of North China Craton's skarn iron ore clusters results from lower La/Yb, ε Nd (t), and AST values, and higher oxygen fugacity. This implies two probable causative mechanisms: first, derivation from a magma source inherently depleted in copper, which is unfavorable for copper mineralization; second, the high oxygen fugacity likely dispersed copper within the iron-dominated ore system, preventing the effective concentration and formation of discrete copper orebodies. This study provides significant new insights for assessing Fe Cu fertility of plutons. Published: 2026-03-04T00:16:03+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.596 (consider)

Relevance: 0.596 (consider)

The North China Craton in eastern China contains several skarn iron ore clusters that are notably copper-deficient compared to those in the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Belt, but their genesis remains unclear. We collected apatite major element and whole rocks major and trace element data from skarn Fe/Cu deposit-associated and barren intermediate–acid plutons in both regions. A comparison of the whole-rock and apatite geochemistry of plutons with different fertility levels (Barren, Fe-fertile, and Cu-bearing) reveals that mineralized plutons result from multiple coupled factors. We employed a Random Forest model to classify pluton fertility (Cu-bearing, Fe-fertile, and barren) based on apatite-equilibrium melt parameters (melt F, Cl, SO 3 , and ΔFMQ). The model, trained on a dataset of 425 apatite analyses (181 Cu-bearing, 154 Fe-fertile, and 90 barren), achieved robust performance with an accuracy, precision, and recall of 0.88, and a five-fold cross-validation F1 score of 0.87 ± 0.02. This analysis reveals distinct geochemical signatures: Barren plutons have lower contents of F Melt , Cl Melt , and SO 3(Melt) . Fe-fertile plutons show the highest Cl Melt and higher ΔFMQ. Conversely, Cu-bearing plutons have higher SO 3(Melt) but lower Cl Melt and ΔFMQ than Fe-fertile plutons. Using an integrated set of apatite and whole-rock indicators, we established diagnostic thresholds: Barren plutons are distinguished by lower median values of La/Yb ratio (17), AST (apatite saturation temperatures; 845 °C), melt F (1180 ppm) and SO 3 (32 ppm) contents in apatite. Cu-bearing plutons are defined by higher median values of La/Yb ratio (35), ε Nd (t) (−6.93), AST (944 °C), melt F (1819 ppm) and SO 3 (64 ppm) contents, and moderate oxidizing conditions (ΔFMQ −1.8 – +2.9). Fe-fertile plutons are characterized by lower median values of La/Yb ratio (16), ε Nd (t) (−13.31), and AST (865 °C), coupled with higher median values of melt F (1637 ppm), Cl (2366 ppm), and SO 3 (66 ppm) contents, and higher oxidizing conditions (ΔFMQ −0.8 – +4.3). These indicators provide a practical tool for identifying Cu-fertile plutons within the North China Craton. Based on these findings, comparative analysis suggests that the copper deficiency in intrusions of North China Craton's skarn iron ore clusters results from lower La/Yb, ε Nd (t), and AST values, and higher oxygen fugacity. This implies two probable causative mechanisms: first, derivation from a magma source inherently depleted in copper, which is unfavorable for copper mineralization; second, the high oxygen fugacity likely dispersed copper within the iron-dominated ore system, preventing the effective concentration and formation of discrete copper orebodies. This study provides significant new insights for assessing Fe Cu fertility of plutons.

item.title: A central position for the South China Block in Rodinia: New evidence from a non-subduction origin of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode mafic–ultramafic rocks
item.dc:creator: Chutian Shu
Xi Zhu
Zheng-Xiang Li
Jingyu Wang
Zhuoying Zhang
Xiaoping Long
item.description: The South China Block (SCB) was formed through the amalgamation of the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks along the Sibao orogen during the early Neoproterozoic assembly of the Rodinia supercontinent. Although possessing records of both the assembly and breakup of Rodinia, the position of the SCB in Rodinia remains debated, mainly due to disagreement on the tectonic settings of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism along the orogen. The magmatism has been interpreted either as subduction-related, placing South China on the margin of Rodinia, or as plume-related, suggesting a central position linking Australia and Laurentia. In this study, we investigate a gabbro-peridotite complex in the central Sibao orogen, with the peridotite showing cumulate textures and the gabbro giving a zircon U Pb crystallization age of 812.8 ± 5.7 Ma. These samples, together with a compilation of 560 published mafic–ultramafic and granitic samples within the 827 ± 22 Ma episode from the Sibao orogen, define cumulate lines of descent characterized by a continuous decline in Mg# with increasing SiO 2 , rather than the Z -shaped Mg#-SiO2 trend diagnostic of hydrous, subduction-related magmas, which is consistent with crystallization from anhydrous melts. This indicates that the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism originated from a dry mantle source, inconsistent with an active subduction setting. Mantle potential temperatures estimated from near-primary high-MgO basalts (MgO up to 17.5 wt%, Mg# up to 75, showing equilibrium with mantle) within this episode exceed 1525 °C, significantly above the ambient mantle potential temperature expected at ~827 Ma (1350–1420 °C), pointing to an anomalously hot mantle source. Initial 187 Os/ 188 Os isotopes of the studied cumulative peridotites (0.1376–0.1642) and coeval mafic–ultramafic rocks (0.1100–0.4191) indicate assimilation-fractional crystallization (AFC) processes involving asthenospheric components (plume), continental lithospheric mantle, and crustal material. This is consistent with plume underplating and interaction with the pre-existing subduction-modified lithosphere and overlying crust. Coeval plume-related magmatism is also documented elsewhere within the South China Block beyond the Sibao orogen, e.g., the Bikou flood basalts, further supporting a regional-scale thermal anomaly. Collectively, these observations are best explained by mantle plume activity, supporting a central position for the South China Block in Rodinia. Published: 2026-03-09T16:40:39+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.592 (consider)

Relevance: 0.592 (consider)

The South China Block (SCB) was formed through the amalgamation of the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks along the Sibao orogen during the early Neoproterozoic assembly of the Rodinia supercontinent. Although possessing records of both the assembly and breakup of Rodinia, the position of the SCB in Rodinia remains debated, mainly due to disagreement on the tectonic settings of the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism along the orogen. The magmatism has been interpreted either as subduction-related, placing South China on the margin of Rodinia, or as plume-related, suggesting a central position linking Australia and Laurentia. In this study, we investigate a gabbro-peridotite complex in the central Sibao orogen, with the peridotite showing cumulate textures and the gabbro giving a zircon U Pb crystallization age of 812.8 ± 5.7 Ma. These samples, together with a compilation of 560 published mafic–ultramafic and granitic samples within the 827 ± 22 Ma episode from the Sibao orogen, define cumulate lines of descent characterized by a continuous decline in Mg# with increasing SiO 2 , rather than the Z -shaped Mg#-SiO2 trend diagnostic of hydrous, subduction-related magmas, which is consistent with crystallization from anhydrous melts. This indicates that the 827 ± 22 Ma episode magmatism originated from a dry mantle source, inconsistent with an active subduction setting. Mantle potential temperatures estimated from near-primary high-MgO basalts (MgO up to 17.5 wt%, Mg# up to 75, showing equilibrium with mantle) within this episode exceed 1525 °C, significantly above the ambient mantle potential temperature expected at ~827 Ma (1350–1420 °C), pointing to an anomalously hot mantle source. Initial 187 Os/ 188 Os isotopes of the studied cumulative peridotites (0.1376–0.1642) and coeval mafic–ultramafic rocks (0.1100–0.4191) indicate assimilation-fractional crystallization (AFC) processes involving asthenospheric components (plume), continental lithospheric mantle, and crustal material. This is consistent with plume underplating and interaction with the pre-existing subduction-modified lithosphere and overlying crust. Coeval plume-related magmatism is also documented elsewhere within the South China Block beyond the Sibao orogen, e.g., the Bikou flood basalts, further supporting a regional-scale thermal anomaly. Collectively, these observations are best explained by mantle plume activity, supporting a central position for the South China Block in Rodinia.

item.title: Role of serpentinization in platinum group element (PGE) mobilization—Evidence from experiments
item.dc:creator: Stefano Tenuta
Katy A. Evans
Steven M. Reddy
David W. Saxey
Denis Fougerouse
Brian Tattitch
item.description: Reducing conditions are typical of incipient serpentinization in which sulfides and alloys break down to form secondary phases. Sulfides and alloys are the main hosts of platinum group elements (PGEs) in serpentinites, and the role of serpentinization in the remobilization of these elements is controversial. Here, we replicated early serpentinization using cold-seal pressure vessels, reacting a mixture of olivine, synthetic laurite, and water at 50 MPa and temperatures of 250−350 °C for durations of 1−4 d. Olivine reacts partially to form serpentine and brucite. Laurite breaks down to form RuSx with variable Ru:S ratios plus euhedral FeS grains. There are no systematic relationships among temperature, time, water content, and the composition of the Ru phase. No neoformed Ru phases were identified, consistent with limited Ru mobility during serpentinization. Silicate mineral assemblages and breakdown of primary laurite are consistent with observations in natural and experimental systems where incipient serpentinization occurred at highly reducing conditions. The results are consistent with decoupling of Ir group PGEs and Pd group PGEs during serpentinization and suggest that serpentinization may affect PGEs differently in different geodynamic settings, with consequences for global PGE cycling. Published: 2026-03-04T21:20:41+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.590 (consider)
